content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 3, 1999


Monty Gonsalves
Post Office Box 1748
Crescent City, California 95531

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-227

Dear Mr. Gonsalves:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please keep in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place.² In addition, this letter is solely based on the facts presented to us in your letter requesting advice, and in the telephone conversation that I had with you on August 10, 1999. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when issuing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Our advice is applicable only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been provided.

QUESTION

May you participate in decisions by the Crescent City Harbor District Commission regarding the lease of harbor district facilities to Eureka Fisheries and Sea Products?

CONCLUSION

You may not participate in decisions regarding the lease of harbor district facilities to Eureka Fisheries or Sea Products.


_________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Pursuant to Regulation 18329, the Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)

File No. A-99-227
Page 2

FACTS

You are an elected member of the Crescent City Harbor District Commission. You are
also a commercial fisherman who wholly owns and operates your own fishing business, that is
worth more than $1,000, and from which you derive more than $250 in income annually.

The Harbor District Commission is in the process of negotiating new agreements for the
lease of harbor district facilities to two fish processors that operate in the Crescent City Harbor -
Eureka Fisheries and Sea Products. Within the last twelve months, you have sold fish to both
Eureka Fisheries and Sea Products, for an amount equal to or exceeding $250. You and the
members of your immediate family have no other connection with either Eureka Fisheries or Sea
Products.

You would like to know whether you are prohibited from participating in decisions by the
Harbor District Commission regarding the new leases because of your past financial dealings
with the fish processors.

ANALYSIS

The conflict of interest provisions of the Act prohibit a public official from making,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the public official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she
has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) As a member of the Crescent City Harbor District
Commission, you are a public official. (Section 82048.)

Whether you have a financial interest in a decision is governed by Section 87103, which
provides, in part, that:

"A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his
or her immediate family, or on any of the following:
(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct
or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more.

***

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in

File No. A-99-227
Page 3

value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management."

Economic Interests

You have advised us that you wholly own and operate your own fishing business, that is
worth $1,000 or more, and from which you derive $250 or more in income annually. Based on
this information, it is apparent that you have an economic interest in your fishing business under
subdivisions (a), (c), and (d) of Section 87103.

Additionally, you have advised us that within the past twelve months, your fishing
business has received $250 or more in income from Eureka Fisheries and from Sea Products for
fish that you have sold to these companies. Under Section 82030(a), the income of an individual
includes a pro rata share of any income of a business entity in which the individual, or his or her
spouse, owns a 10 percent interest or greater. As you wholly own your fishing business, the
income received by your fishing business from these companies is all considered to be income to
you. You therefore also have an economic interest in both Eureka Fisheries and Sea Products,
under subdivision (c) of Section 87103, as sources of income to you.

Once a public official's economic interests have been identified, it is necessary to
evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on any of the economic interests that have been identified. There are three steps
to making this evaluation. First, it must be determined whether the official's economic interests
will be directly or indirectly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) Second, the
appropriate standard must be selected for determining whether the financial impact of the
decision on any particular economic interest will be material. (Regulation 18700(b)(5).) Third,
it must be determined whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be
satisfied for any particular economic interest. (Regulation 18700(b)(6).) If it is substantially
likely that the materiality standard will be satisfied for any of the official's economic interests,
then the public official will have a conflict of interest, unless the "public generally exception"
applies. If it is not substantially likely that the materiality standard will be satisfied for any of the
official's economic interests, then the public official will not have a conflict of interest. We
stress that this is a case-by-case determination.

Direct Versus Indirect Involvement

Regulation 18704.1 sets forth the criteria for determining whether an economic interest is
directly or indirectly involved in a decision. This regulation states:

"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income,
and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an

File No. A-99-227
Page 4

official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person."

We have been given no indication that your fishing business would satisfy any of the
above-stated criteria for being considered directly involved in decisions by the Harbor District
Commission regarding the leasing of harbor district facilities to Eureka Fisheries or Sea
Products. Accordingly, the involvement of your fishing business in decisions by the Harbor
District Commission regarding new leases with Eureka Fisheries and Sea Products would only be
indirect.

By contrast, Eureka Fisheries and Sea Products are named parties in, and the subjects of
decisions by the Harbor District Commission regarding the new leases. As such, your economic
interests in these two companies, as sources of income to you, are directly involved in the Harbor
District Commission decisions.

The Appropriate Materiality Standard

Regulation 18705.1(b) prescribes the rules for assessing whether an official's economic
interest in a business entity, such as your fishing business, that is only indirectly involved in a
decision, is materially affected by the decision. The rules prescribed in the regulation are
alternative rules. Which rule applies to a particular business entity is dependent on the size of the
business entity. We assume that the rule set forth in subsection (b)(7) is the rule that applies to
your fishing business, based on your description of the size of that business. Subsection (b)(7)
provides:

"The effect of a decision is material as to a business entity in
which an official has an economic interest if any of the following
applies:

***

"(7) For any business entity not covered by subdivisions (b)(1)
through (b)(6), inclusive:
"(A) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
gross revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing

File No. A-99-227
Page 5

expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
"(C) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more."

However, when a public official has an economic interest in a business entity, because the
business entity is a source of income to the official, and the business entity is directly involved in
a decision, as is the case with Eureka Fisheries and Sea Products, any reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on the business entity flowing from the decision is considered material.
(Regulation 18705.3.) This is a "one-penny" rule.

Foreseeability

Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a particular decision by the Harbor District
Commission about reaching new lease agreements with Eureka Fisheries or Sea Products will
have a material effect on your fishing business, Eureka Fisheries, or Sea Products is the critical
question in this analysis. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable at the time a
government decision is made if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation
18706.) Certainty is not required. Only if an effect is just a mere possibility, is it not reasonably
foreseeable. (Downey Cares v. Downey Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822; and In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
198.)

You have not provided us with specific information about any particular decision that you
would be called upon to make regarding new lease agreements with either Eureka Fisheries or
Sea Products. We are therefore unable to determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that any
of these decisions will have an economic effect on your fishing business that is sufficient to
satisfy one or more of the materiality thresholds described in subdivision (b)(7) of Regulation
18705.1(b).

Nonetheless, it is a virtual certainty that any decision made by the Harbor District
Commission regarding a new lease agreement with Eureka Fisheries would have some economic
effect on Eureka Fisheries. This would be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
your economic interest in Eureka Fisheries. Similarly, it is a virtual certainty that any decision
made by the Harbor District Commission regarding a new lease agreement with Sea Products
would have some economic effect on Sea Products. This would be a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on your economic interest in Sea Products. Therefore, you may not
participate in any decision by the Harbor District Commission regarding new lease agreements
with either Eureka Fisheries or Sea Products, unless the "public generally" exception applies to
the decision.

Public Generally

For the "public generally" exception to apply to a decision, the decision must affect the
official's interest in substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant segment of the

File No. A-99-227
Page 6

public. (Regulation 18707.) Regulation 18707(b)(1)(B) defines the term "significant segment," as it relates to an economic interest in a business entity, as "fifty percent of all businesses in the jurisdiction or the district the official represents, so long as the segment is composed of persons other than a single industry, trade, or profession."

Under the facts presented to us, it appears unlikely that a decision by the Harbor District Commission regarding new lease agreements with Eureka Fisheries or Sea Products would affect fifty percent of all businesses in Crescent City, or any particular district in Crescent City that you may represent, in substantially the same manner as Eureka Fisheries or Sea Products. It therefore appears that you are prohibited from participating in decisions by the Harbor District Commission regarding the leases.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]

By:     Steven Benito Russo
         Staff Counsel, Legal Division
